edibility: Too tough to eat raw; traditionally consumed as a bitter tea or decoction for its medicinal properties. Has been used in traditional medicine for centuries, particularly in Asian herbalism.
taste: Bitter and woody, characteristic of the decoction. The flavor is astringent and earthy.
search: Found year-round as perennial conks on dead or weakened hardwoods, particularly birch and oak. Identifies by the distinctive concentric zones and chalky white underside when fresh. Look-alikes include other Ganoderma species; Artist's Conk is distinguished by its flattened, shelf-like fruiting body and lack of a stem.
